chapters.title: Was the past better?
chapters.text:
No one can question a parent's love for their children, but the expression of this love varies greatly. Our environment profoundly influences us, and parenting is not exempt. While caretakers strive to assist children in learning to talk, walk, and thrive in life, some have gone to extreme lengths, leaving them with no room to grow. Whatever happened to the days when scraped knees were part of learning to ride a bike?

Nowadays, doting parents can't help but keep a constant eye on their children. However, it wasn’t always like this. Before the ‘80s, children learned autonomy early in life, which helped them handle adversity later on. Baby Boomers (born between 1945 and 1964) and Generation X (born between 1965 and 1980) fall into this “early grownups” category. Nevertheless, the Baby Boomers changed the status quo with their Millennial children, while Gen X followed suit with their Gen Z kids. But why did this change happen?

Parenting is imprinting ourselves onto our kids.

We can trace this shift in parenting style to the ideas that gained popularity between 1983 and 1984. The widespread news about child abduction made parents acutely aware of the dangers posed by strangers. While the education system highlighted declining literacy rates in America, the self-esteem movement emerged, emphasizing individuality more than academic achievement. This societal shift has resulted in the rise of Millennials and a participation trophy mindset, leading to an unmotivated generation that values minimum effort.

If this resonates with your own experiences or those of your parents, follow us deep down the parenthood iceberg. Our upbringing can reveal a lot about ourselves, so if you intend to become a parent, why not take this opportunity to make informed choices from the beginning? Shall we start?

chapters.title: Aboard the parenting helicopter
chapters.text:
While Millennials may have been the first “helicoptered generation,” their Boomer parents were definitely not the pioneers of overcaring. The legend says that back in 1899, the mother of General Douglas McArthur, a renowned leader in American history, moved to West Point Academy to oversee his studies. The tradition resurfaced in the late ‘90s when Millennials started college.

Lythcott-Haims vividly remembers the unexpected surge of parents on the campus of Stanford University at the time. The incoming first-year students depended on their parents to solve problems, make decisions, and seek opportunities for them. This behavior became so apparent that those students were soon dubbed “college kids” instead of college students.

Let’s take a closer look at the Boomers to understand their motivations. Firstly, we find that the sheer size of their group — 76 million as of 1980 — made them involuntary trendsetters. Boomers did not shy away from challenging the status quo as their parents barked orders at them while their grandparents preferred them to be silent.

In opposition to this, they decided to parent differently. Unlike the emotional indifference and strictness they grew up in, this new generation of parents tried to be more open with their children, often becoming their best friends and advisors. While their parents maintained a negligent approach, Boomers established much closer supervision over their kids. In other words, society transitioned from emotionally distant to emotionally present parenting and from a hands-off approach to a more active and controlling one.

On top of that, hierarchy and structure replaced adherence, while the sexual revolution gave way to authority. As family dynamics gradually shifted, they also affected societal processes. Dual-income-earning households gained popularity, leading to a steep increase in divorce rates and a greater emphasis on spending quality time with kids.

Maybe those champions of self-actualization, the Boomers, did so much for their kids that their kids have been robbed of a chance to develop a belief in their own selves. ~ Julie Lythcott-Haims

Julie Lythcott-Haims

Boomers carried on with their tendency to challenge authority and champion free thinking. But as time passed and their kids became parents, the problem with Millennials' upbringing jumped out. Like their Boomer caretakers, they pursued a similar parental vision but took it to an extreme with the “helicopter parenting” tradition.

The long-term effect of helicopter parenting is existential incompetence on the part of the kids.

chapters.title: Too much care is no care at all
chapters.text:
We all want our children to be successful and respected in life. However, when the helicopter view enters the frame, they are more likely to succeed despite the excessive care rather than because of it. In truth, helicopter parents unintentionally deprive their children of the space needed to express themselves and grow their self-efficacy.

Speaking of self-efficacy, the creative mindset is an integral part of it, especially in times of rapid technological advancement. Coined by famous psychologist Albert Bandura, self-efficacy is a person's belief in their ability to plan and perform effectively to show promising outcomes. In other words, we can't succeed without taking risks, and overwhelming parenting usually keeps children from doing exactly that.

Upbringing implies growth, but parental fears can hinder the child's organic development.

Ironically, the families that are likely to indulge in helicopter parenting are the ones who can afford a little flying apparatus. Jokes aside, middle and upper-middle-class parents struggle the most to get their kids off their minds. With more financial flexibility, they can afford to spend time with their children. However, irrational worries can have side effects, leading parents to invade their kids' personal space. This negatively impacts children and takes a toll on the mental well-being of adults, who, driven by fear, find overparenting emotionally draining. As a result, neither party has time for themselves, despite their undeniable love for each other.

Biologically, parents are wired to keep their child safe, but today's overcaring tendency turned this instinct into hypervigilance. Children must explore the world, with its dangers and risks, to become self-efficient for themselves and others. Despite our desire to shield them from all evil, our competency ends where kids' privacy begins. It's a harsh truth, but there are ways to accommodate it.

Next time you feel like overprotecting your child, ask yourself these three questions:

• To what end?

Usually, parents are afraid not of the actual harm but of what might happen, which can make them paranoid. Such behavior can ruin the parent-child relationship, and it’s essential to remember this. Not only that, but kids can also develop the "stranger danger syndrome," causing them to avoid necessary human interaction after being taught to be wary of all strangers.

• How can I teach necessary skills to my child while protecting them?

Remember, the more your child can explore their independence and the outside world, the less likely they are to become victims due to inexperience.

• How can I teach them to be autonomous?

A less-structured and checkboxed childhood leads to the development of a highly capable adult. Repeat this truth as a mantra and always keep it in mind.

chapters.title: Shielding the kid or are you kidding yourself?
chapters.text:
There is no doubt that parenting is a responsibility no one's ever truly ready for, regardless of preparation. And it's even harder to act composed when dealing with teenagers and young adults. As they develop more autonomy, the worry they cause to their parents also scales. That said, it's difficult to pass judgment on parents who only have one chance to do things right. Still, it's impossible not to notice some ridiculous measures people use to propel their children into life.

Let's start with schools. Overprotective parents research the facilities their children will study at, the sports they will do, and even how they will get into college. It's common knowledge that extracurriculars — sports, clubs, private tutors — help students gain admission to the most prestigious colleges.

As a result, health providers warn about the potential physical risks of practicing one sport instead of several — according to research, such specialization can lead to overtraining and acute traumas. Studies also show that intense engagement in individual sports continues to increase despite these health risks.

While obsessing over a child's academic success completely overlooks risks for physical health, butting in their primary responsibilities undermines their autonomy.

Helicopter parenting is a double-edged sword: it helps keep children safe but can endanger their long-term well-being and potential.

Stepping into their children's shoes, parents rarely understand the detrimental risks they impose on their future. As a result, many late adolescents today struggle with:

• Emotional stability
• Social confidence
• Self-efficacy
• Trust in peers
• Socialization
• Academic performance

Today, caretakers don't shy away from doing school assignments, securing high test scores on behalf of their children, and concealing their child's misdemeanors like alcohol and drug consumption. In other words, they avoid holding their kids accountable for their actions, ultimately raising future adults with zero judgment, social naivete, and poor resilience.

In addition, helicopter parenting has jeopardized young people's mental health. A 2022 survey revealed a direct link between overprotection and symptoms of depression and anxiety. Often, parents set undue expectations for their children, increasing the risk of distress, self-harm, and even thoughts of suicide. As a result, children have no space to explore and create the life path they want; instead, they suffocate under the relentless demands of their achievement-obsessed parents.

There are two things children should get from their parents: roots and wings. ~ Johann Wolfgang von Goethe

Julie Lythcott-Haims

Did you know? Social and economic crises caused the “suspended adulthood” phenomenon, where young adults prolong youthful experiences instead of pursuing careers.

chapters.title: Instead of raising children, we should raise adults
chapters.text:
When Lythcott-Haims talks about “raising adults,” what does she mean? When do children enter adulthood, and what comes with the new status? The legal adult can marry regardless of parental opinion, serve in the army, and drink alcohol. But the Constitution doesn't define the behavioral side of adulthood. A few decades ago, growing up coincided with societal norms: graduating, leaving home, earning good money, tying the knot, and having children.

But times have certainly changed. In the 1960s, most people checked all these boxes by age thirty, but the trend started declining since the beginning of the 21st century, indicating that traditional milestones have outlived themselves. Modern young adults realize marriage doesn't have to coincide with parenting and vice versa. These outdated demands also adhere to hetero-conforming norms, disregarding gay marriages that are still illegal in many countries. To address these changes and shifts, we need a new definition of adulthood that encompasses psychological milestones.

Today, we consider a person an adult if they:

• Have an equal and healthy-distance relationship with their parents.
• Hold clear values and beliefs.
• Are financially emancipated from their caretakers.
• Take responsibility for their actions.

If an adult child isn't fully capable of these skills, some may assume they're not mature enough. Lythcott-Haims shares a story of a college freshman who was so used to his mother doing errands for him that he didn't know how to ask for help carrying heavy parcels from home until his mother phoned someone from the campus. This extreme example illustrates the skills that young adults must obtain to sashay confidently in life. Among other things, they must be able to:

• Communicate with new people: faculty staff, landlords, bank workers, or bus drivers.
• Navigate the campus and to a new town if they move.
• Handle their assignments and deadlines responsibly.
• Do their chores around the household when living with others.
• Solve personal problems without parental involvement.
• Be considerate of the ups and downs in life and manage them successfully.
• Earn their own money and manage it mindfully.
• Take calculated risks.

Ironically, most parents guard their kids from learning these life essentials. They teach kids not to talk to strangers, drive them around, and constantly remind them of deadlines. But as long as parents can slowly release the grip of self-imposed responsibilities, they'll relieve their minds enormously. But if they worry that the kids will freak out, they should let them take on more responsibilities for better “amortization.”

The future of overcaring is living two lives — yours and your child’s.

chapters.title: It’s time for Peter Pan to leave Neverland
chapters.text:
Those who grew up loving a mischievous flying boy may dislike this harsh advice : don't let your kids become Peter Pans. Remember what happened to Peter Pan when he refused to leave Neverland? After some time, he lost track of time and forgot his parents and where he came from.

By allowing our kids to stay in a carefree childhood for too long, we turn them into domestic Peter Pans, unable to live in society. In other words, the lingering child results from our disproportionate efforts. Indeed, if we want to raise adults, we need to adjust our approach accordingly. However, different parenting styles have emerged due to the diverse upbringing and generational differences among parents. The question arises: Do we have to choose one specific style?

Balanced parenting is giving children what they need on top of what they want.

In 1967, prominent psychologist Diana Baumrind highlighted three types of caretaking:

1. Permissive
2. Authoritarian
3. Authoritative

Sixteen years later, her colleagues John Martin and Eleanor Maccoby revisited the classification, adding another type — neglectful.

The permissive, or indulgent, type describes parents who satisfy their children’s needs and requests and are undemanding overall. Because they established a “no discipline” climate, it's difficult for such parents to say no or apply a penalty. Permissive parents prioritize a friendship-like relationship over their role as educators, resulting in a shallow presence in their kids’ life.

Authoritarian parents are the opposite; unlike indulgent parents, they have high demands yet never explain their reasoning for punishment. “Because I said so” is the typical response that entails discipline and self-control with little freedom. On the other side of receptivity are authoritative parents. They set high expectations and borders for their children, yet they also give feedback and resonate with their kids' emotional needs. In authoritative households, freedom of exploration is present to the fullest.

Finally, the neglectful type of parenting is devoid of both demands and feedback. Usually, such an attitude comes down to a “hands-off” upbringing. They have zero involvement in the child's school and personal life and often can't provide food, clothing, or a place to live.

Helicopter parenting combines authoritarian and indulgent types, two poles of one swing. What we need is a balance provided by authoritative parenting. Children must have responsibilities to feel like a contributive part of family and community, but also rights to personal freedom and non-judgment.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Every mature adult needs essential skills to propel in life. However, these skills don't appear out of the blue but develop throughout the child’s formative years. Children can't cultivate these skills by themselves ; that's where parents come into play. Their main missions as caretakers are to teach the kids to take care of and stand up for themselves and make independent choices.

First, they must find a balance between being too strict and lenient, which is difficult when they were raised on one end of the pole. Parenting has changed a lot in the last fifty years, ranging from demanding and unresponsive to indulgent and reactive.

Claiming to reverse their parents’ cold upbringing, baby boomers went on a crusade to engage in their children’s lives more. But without proper boundaries, they started a snowball of helicopter parenting; what we see today is a problem that wasn't solved in their time. The excessive parental presence in children's lives caused their helplessness and naivete in real life. Some may say mothers and fathers became their kids, living their lives instead of their own.

The mental toll overparenting has on young people is not to be underestimated; psychologists have already noted an increase in anxiety and depression. Home is supposed to be safe; ironically, too much care leads to poor privacy and a lack of personal freedom. Without it, future adults won't manage to build healthy boundaries and create a good life for themselves. So next time you think of calling your freshman ten times a day, remember the potential consequences. Regardless of the parenting style you grew up with, now you can build a perfect home life and upbringing for your children — use this information well.

Try this


1. Practice the “one reminder” policy. Mention the chore once and let the child develop responsibility.
2. Don't fix kids' mistakes. Let them fail and gain experience.
3. Be emotionally open: name your feelings and process them together.

Read it till the end?
